text.title: AI Incident Response Playbook Core Rules
text.summary: Four testable rules every application of AI Incident Response Playbook enforces. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Every step in the AI Incident Response Playbook runbook has explicit pre-conditions, action, post-conditions, and an elapsed-time budget. No vague verbs ('investigate', 'check').

rationale.source: SRE practice / Google SRE Workbook, Ch. 8

rule.rationale: Vague verbs are abandoned during incidents — on-callers can't decide what 'investigate' means at 3am.

rule.statement: Decision branches name the input signal (metric, log line, alert ID) and the threshold that triggers each branch.
rule.rationale: Branches written as 'if it looks wrong' route by mood. Numeric thresholds route by data.

rule.statement: At least one drill of the full runbook is recorded within 90 days. Runbooks never drilled are theater.
rule.rationale: Untested runbooks degrade silently — credentials rotate, scripts break, owners leave.

rule.statement: Escalation triggers and contacts are at the TOP of the runbook, not buried at the bottom.
rule.rationale: Under stress the on-caller scrolls to the top. Burying the escalation path costs minutes.

example.title: Step with input signal + threshold + action
example.code: Step 3: Check error rate on /api/inference over last 5 min If rate >2% AND p95 latency >3s → execute kill-switch (Step 5) Else → continue to Step 4
example.note: Signal, threshold, and routing are explicit. The on-caller cannot guess wrong.

example.title: Vague step
example.code: Step 3: Check if things look bad
example.note: Under stress 'look bad' resolves to whatever mood the on-caller has. Bad triage.
